subject: ap-calculus-ab
theme: Differential equations describe how a quantity changes in relation to itself — the exponential model $\frac{dy}{dt} = ky$ governs growth and decay, separation of variables turns a differential equation into two separate integrals, and slope fields visualize solution behavior without solving explicitly.

core_concepts.title: Differential Equations and Solutions
core_concepts.detail: A differential equation is an equation involving a function and one or more of its derivatives; a solution is a function $y = f(x)$ that satisfies the equation.

core_concepts.title: General vs. Particular Solutions
core_concepts.detail: A general solution to $\frac{dy}{dx} = f(x)$ contains an arbitrary constant $C$; a particular solution fixes $C$ by applying an initial condition $y(x_0) = y_0$.

core_concepts.title: Separation of Variables
core_concepts.detail: Separation of variables: if $\frac{dy}{dx} = g(x)h(y)$, rewrite as $\frac{dy}{h(y)} = g(x)\,dx$, then integrate both sides separately.

core_concepts.title: Exponential Growth and Decay Model
core_concepts.detail: The exponential model: $\frac{dy}{dt} = ky$ has the general solution $y = Ce^{kt}$, where $k > 0$ models growth and $k < 0$ models decay.

core_concepts.title: Slope Fields
core_concepts.detail: A slope field (direction field) is a graphical plot of short line segments with slope $f'(x,y)$ at each point — it shows the shape of solution curves without solving the differential equation.

core_concepts.title: Sketching Solutions on Slope Fields
core_concepts.detail: To sketch a particular solution on a slope field, start at the initial condition point and follow the slope field arrows, staying tangent to the segments.

core_concepts.title: Doubling Time and Half-Life
core_concepts.detail: Doubling time (growth) occurs when $e^{kt} = 2$, giving $t = \frac{\ln 2}{k}$. Half-life (decay) occurs when $e^{kt} = \frac{1}{2}$, giving $t = \frac{\ln 2}{|k|}$.

core_concepts.title: AB vs. BC Scope Warning
core_concepts.detail: AB-scope warning: integration by parts (6.11), partial fractions (6.12), Euler's method (7.5), and logistic models (7.9) are BC-only and will NOT appear on the AB exam.

formulas.name: Separation of Variables
formulas.description: Rearrange the differential equation so that all y terms (including dy) are on one side and all x terms (including dx) are on the other. Then integrate both sides. Don't forget the constant C — it goes on one side only.
formulas.example: $\frac{dy}{dx} = 3y$: separate to $\frac{dy}{y} = 3\,dx$. Integrate: $\ln|y| = 3x + C$. Solve: $y = Ae^{3x}$ (where $A = e^C$).

formulas.name: Exponential Growth/Decay Model
formulas.description: Any quantity whose rate of change is proportional to the quantity itself grows or decays exponentially. The constant $k$ is the growth rate ($k > 0$) or decay rate ($k < 0$). $C$ is the initial value when $t = 0$.
formulas.example: Bacteria double every 3 hours, so $k = \frac{\ln 2}{3}$. Starting with 500 bacteria: $y = 500e^{(\ln 2/3)t}$. At $t = 9$: $y = 500 \cdot 2^3 = 4000$.

formulas.name: Particular Solution (Initial Value Problem)
formulas.katex_string: y = y_0 e^{k(t - t_0)} \quad \text{when } y(t_0) = y_0
formulas.description: If you know the value of $y$ at a specific time $t_0$, substitute into the general solution to solve for $C$. Alternatively, use this direct formula: the solution passing through $(t_0, y_0)$. Used whenever a specific starting value is given.

formulas.name: Half-Life Formula
formulas.description: The time required for an exponentially decaying quantity to reach half its current value. Derived by setting $y = y_0/2$ and solving $e^{kt} = 1/2$. The same formula (with $k$ positive) gives doubling time for growth.

exam_tip: When solving an IVP on AP free-response, always show the full separation of variables step by step: separate, integrate both sides, solve for y, then substitute the initial condition to find C. Jumping straight to the answer loses justification points — College Board wants to see each algebraic step.
